behaviors.description: SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of values in the referenced range
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure — wrong sum result — lives entirely within the range-expansion logic and the summation function. No module boundary crossing is needed; the evaluator receives a crafted in-memory grid and returns a result. REAL-INFRA: no OS/network. LLM-DEP: no. MULTI-STEP: no. INTEGRATION-CHECK down-test passes: calling evaluate('=SUM(A1:A10)') with ten in-memory cell values is a single function invocation with crafted arguments.
behaviors.test_description: Populate an in-memory cell grid with cells A1–A10 set to known numeric values (e.g., 1–10). Call the formula evaluator on a cell containing =SUM(A1:A10). Assert the result equals 55. Also test with mixed types (text cells should be ignored or treated as 0 per spec) and with an empty range.

behaviors.description: A formula referencing another formula cell cascades evaluation correctly (e.g., =B1+1 where B1 is =A1*2)
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure appears specifically when the formula evaluator hands a cell reference to the cell resolver, which must re-invoke the evaluator on the referenced cell's own formula — a cross-module recursive call. A unit test of evaluate() in isolation cannot expose a bug in how the evaluator delegates to the resolver and back. REAL-INFRA: no. LLM-DEP: no. MULTI-STEP: no. INTEGRATION-CHECK: down-test fails — the bug lives at the handoff between the formula evaluator module and the cell-reference resolver module, not inside either one alone. Up-test is not needed; in-process wiring suffices.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the formula evaluator and cell-reference resolver together in-process. Set A1=5, B1='=A1*2', and a target cell C1='=B1+1'. Call the integrated engine to evaluate C1. Assert the result is 11. Also verify that changing A1 to 3 and re-evaluating C1 returns 7, confirming the chain re-resolves rather than caching stale values.

behaviors.description: The engine detects circular dependencies and returns a circular reference error instead of looping
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Cycle detection is a graph algorithm (DFS with a visited/in-stack set) applied to the dependency graph. The failure — infinite loop or stack overflow — is triggered entirely within the evaluate() call on crafted cells; no module handoff is required to expose it. REAL-INFRA: no. LLM-DEP: no. INTEGRATION-CHECK down-test passes: set A1='=B1' and B1='=A1', call evaluate(A1), and assert a circular-reference error is returned. One function call, crafted arguments.
behaviors.test_description: Build an in-memory grid with A1='=B1' and B1='=A1'. Call the evaluator on A1. Assert it returns a circular-reference error (not a value, not an infinite loop). Also test a longer cycle (A1→B1→C1→A1) and a self-reference (A1='=A1'). Confirm that non-cyclic cells in the same grid still evaluate normally.

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP returns the correct value on exact match and an error when the key is absent
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: VLOOKUP's correctness — lookup strategy, match mode flag, column extraction — is entirely within the function's algorithm. The failure (wrong value or wrong error) appears inside the function itself, not at a module boundary. REAL-INFRA: no. LLM-DEP: no. INTEGRATION-CHECK down-test passes: call vlookup(lookup_value, table_range, col_index, exact_match=True) with a crafted in-memory table and assert the result.
behaviors.test_description: Construct an in-memory 4×3 lookup table. Call VLOOKUP with a key that exists in column 1 and assert the correct column-3 value is returned. Call again with a key not in the table and assert a #N/A error. Test boundary cases: key in the first row, key in the last row, duplicate keys (should return the first match), and an out-of-bounds column index.

behaviors.description: The IF function correctly evaluates nested conditions and returns the right branch
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Nested IF evaluation is a recursive parse-and-dispatch problem entirely within the expression evaluator. The failure (wrong branch selected) lives inside the IF handler's condition evaluation logic. REAL-INFRA: no. LLM-DEP: no. INTEGRATION-CHECK down-test passes: call evaluate('=IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low")') with A1 set to various values in memory.
behaviors.test_description: Set A1 to 5, 15, and 25 in separate test cases and evaluate =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low"). Assert results are "low", "medium", and "high" respectively. Also test the exact boundary values (A1=10 → "low", A1=20 → "medium") and a nested IF in the false branch to confirm both branches recurse correctly.

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins non-empty cell values and skips empty cells rather than inserting blank strings
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The skipping-empty-cells behavior is a conditional inside the CONCATENATE function's argument-iteration loop. The failure (blank string inserted vs. skipped) appears within the function itself when it receives an empty-cell value. REAL-INFRA: no. LLM-DEP: no. INTEGRATION-CHECK down-test passes: call concatenate([val1, "", val2], separator) directly with crafted argument list including empty strings.
behaviors.test_description: Call CONCATENATE with a mix of string values and empty strings (representing empty cells) as arguments, using a comma separator. Assert that empty-string arguments are omitted from the output (e.g., ["a", "", "b"] → "a,b" not "a,,b"). Also verify: all-empty input returns an empty string, single non-empty value returns that value without separator, and that a cell containing only whitespace is NOT skipped (whitespace ≠ empty).

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet (e.g., =Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when that sheet exists
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure — wrong value or unresolved reference — appears at the boundary between the formula parser (recognizing Sheet2!A1 as a cross-sheet token) and the sheet registry/resolver (looking up the correct sheet by name and fetching the cell). Neither component alone produces this bug; it requires both to hand off data correctly. REAL-INFRA: no. LLM-DEP: no. INTEGRATION-CHECK: down-test fails — no single function with crafted args exercises the parser→registry→resolver chain. Up-test not needed; in-process wiring with two sheet objects is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the formula parser, sheet registry, and cell resolver together in-process. Create two in-memory sheets: Sheet1 and Sheet2. Set Sheet2!A1=10. Evaluate =Sheet2!A1+1 in Sheet1 and assert the result is 11. Also test: referenced sheet does not exist → #REF! error; cross-sheet reference inside SUM (=SUM(Sheet2!A1:Sheet2!A3)); and a cross-sheet chain (Sheet1 references Sheet2 which references Sheet3).

behaviors.description: When a cell's value changes, all dependent formulas are recalculated in the correct topological order
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Topological ordering of recalculation requires the dependency tracker, the dirty-marking mechanism, and the evaluator to interact: the tracker must know the DAG, the propagation must mark cells dirty in order, and the evaluator must re-evaluate in the correct sequence. The failure (stale intermediate value because B1 was evaluated before A1 propagated) only appears when these three modules are wired together. REAL-INFRA: no. LLM-DEP: no. INTEGRATION-CHECK: down-test fails; in-process multi-module wiring is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a three-cell chain in-process: A1=2, B1='=A1*3', C1='=B1+1'. Evaluate all cells (C1=7). Then change A1 to 5 and trigger recalculation. Assert B1=15 and C1=16 after propagation. Instrument or inspect the recalculation order to confirm B1 is recomputed before C1. Also test a diamond dependency (A1→B1, A1→C1, B1+C1→D1) and verify D1 is correct after A1 changes.

behaviors.description: Undo after a formula edit restores both the formula text and all previously computed dependent values
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure — formula text restored but dependents still show stale post-edit values — appears only when the undo stack, the formula store, and the recalculation engine are wired together. The undo stack restoring formula text is one module; the cascade recalculation that must follow is another. A unit test of either module alone cannot expose the missing recalculation triggered from undo. REAL-INFRA: no. LLM-DEP: no. INTEGRATION-CHECK: down-test fails; up-test not needed; in-process wiring is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the undo stack, formula store, and evaluator together in-process. Set A1=5, B1='=A1*2' (evaluates to 10). Edit B1 to '=A1*3' (evaluates to 15). Call undo(). Assert B1's formula is '=A1*2' and B1's computed value is 10. Also test: undo of a formula that has downstream dependents (C1='=B1+1' should revert to 11), redo re-applies the edit correctly, and multiple sequential undos unwind the full edit history.

behaviors.description: Saving a workbook to disk and reloading it produces identical cell values and formulas with no precision loss
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The serializer and deserializer must agree on binary format encoding; neither can be tested alone. The failure (precision loss or formula corruption) appears at the round-trip boundary between the serializer and deserializer modules. REAL-INFRA: Writing to a temp file touches the OS filesystem, but the key failure mode — floating-point precision loss in the custom binary format — is reproducible using an in-process byte buffer (BytesIO), so OS filesystem behavior is not the failure boundary. INTEGRATION-CHECK: down-test with BytesIO reproduces the failure; up-test not needed.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a workbook in-process with: integer cells, floating-point cells with many significant digits (e.g., 1.0/3.0), formula cells ('=A1*2'), and cells with text. Serialize to a BytesIO buffer. Deserialize from the same buffer. Assert every cell value is bit-for-bit identical to the original (use exact float equality since the format is supposed to be lossless). Also write to a real temp file and reload to confirm the code path actually touches the filesystem; assert the same round-trip fidelity.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The in-process BytesIO path is sufficient to catch precision/format bugs (Integration). The temp-file sub-case is included to smoke-test file-handle plumbing, but does not push the minimum level to System because the critical failure mode is serialization logic, not OS behavior.
